content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are playing a game with an array AAA of NNN integers. The rules of the game are as follows:


Let ∣A∣|A|∣A∣ denote the current length of AAA.
If ∣A∣≤2|A| \le 2∣A∣≤2, the game terminates immediately.

Otherwise, Alice must choose an index iii such that 1<i<∣A∣1 < i < |A|1<i<∣A∣.
Then, Bob must choose **one** of the following two actions:

Delete all elements at indices 1,2,…,i1, 2, \dots, i1,2,…,i.
Delete all elements at indices i,i+1,…,∣A∣i, i+1, \dots, |A|i,i+1,…,∣A∣.


After Bob's choice, the remaining elements are re-indexed starting from 111.
After the game terminates, Alice's score is defined to be the largest element remaining in the array.

Note that the game is only allowed to end when there are no more than two elements remaining in AAA.

Alice wants to maximize her score, while Bob wants to minimize Alice's score.

Assuming both players play optimally, find Alice's final score.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input:

The first line contains a single integer NNN, denoting the initial size of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a single integer representing Alice's score under optimal play.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The array's length is already ≤2\le 2≤2, so no moves can be performed.
Alice's score is max⁡(1,2)=2\max(1, 2) = 2max(1,2)=2.
Test case 222: Alice's only possible move is to choose i=2i = 2i=2.
Bob must then delete either [A1,A2][A_1, A_2][A1​,A2​] or [A2,A3][A_2, A_3][A2​,A3​].
He will choose to delete [A2,A3][A_2, A_3][A2​,A3​], leaving the array to be just [1][1][1]. Alice's score is hence 111.
Test case 333: Alice's optimal move is to choose i=3i = 3i=3.
Bob must now delete either the prefix [1,3,3][1, 3, 3][1,3,3] and leave [2,5][2, 5][2,5] (giving Alice a score of max⁡(2,5)=5\max(2, 5) = 5max(2,5)=5), or delete the suffix [3,2,5][3, 2, 5][3,2,5] and leave [1,3][1, 3][1,3] (giving Alice a score of max⁡(1,3)=3\max(1, 3) = 3max(1,3)=3).
Bob wants to minimize Alice's score, so he will choose the second option, leaving Alice with a score of 333.
It can be verified that Alice's other starting moves cannot attain a score larger than 333.

If she chooses i=2i = 2i=2 on her first move, Bob will delete the suffix [3,3,2,5][3, 3, 2, 5][3,3,2,5] leaving the array [1][1][1] with a score of 111.
If she chooses i=4i = 4i=4 on her first move, Bob will delete the suffix [2,5][2, 5][2,5] and make the array [1,3,3][1, 3, 3][1,3,3].
Here, Alice is forced to choose the middle element, and Bob can delete [3,3][3, 3][3,3] leaving Alice with a score of 111 again.
